content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


June 25, 2002


Linda L. Daube
Law Offices of Linda L. Daube
454 West Napa Street, Suite 200
Sonoma, CA 95476-6519

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-02-161

Dear Ms. Daube:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Mayor Frank Aiello regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since you have not identified a specific decision pending before Mayor Aiello, we can only provide you with informal assistance in response to your request.²

QUESTION

Does Frank Aiello, the mayor of the City of Pittsburg, have a conflict of interest disqualifying him from voting on projects proposed by developer Albert Seeno Jr.?

CONCLUSION

Based on the facts you supply, Mayor Aiello does not have a disqualifying conflict of interest and may participate in city council votes on development projects, including final vesting maps and development agreements proposed by Albert Seeno Jr. Mr. Seeno is not a source of income to Mayor Aiello, nor does he otherwise constitute a potentially disqualifying economic interest to him under the Act.


¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3) – copy enclosed.)

File No. I-02-161
Page No. 2

FACTS

In December 1999, Mayor Aiello purchased a new home in the Highland Ranch
subdivision from West Coast Builders, a Seeno-owned company. Mayor Aiello first
approached West Coast Builders for financing on the home and was referred by a
representative of the Seeno company to Bob Ferrero, president of Ham Radio Outlet
("HRO"). Inc as a potential source for a private loan. Bob Ferrero is a long-standing
friend of Mr. Aiello's father. Subsequently, HRO, Inc., through its profit sharing plan,
issued the Aiello family a private loan for Mr. Aiello's purchase of this new home.

All payments on the home loan are directed to HRO, Inc. HRO, Inc. is the only
entity entitled to foreclose in the event of delinquent payments. Mayor Aiello lists the
loan from HRO, Inc. on his Statement of Economic Interests (FPPC Form 700). Mayor
Aiello is unaware of any facts suggesting that HRO, Inc. is legally affiliated in any
manner with West Coast Builders or any other of Mr. Seeno's companies.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard
analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest
(regulation 18700, subdivisions (b)(1) – (8)), which is discussed below.

1. & 2. Is Mr. Aiello a public official? Is he making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?

The conflict-of-interest prohibition applies only to public officials. As the mayor
of the City of Pittsburg and an elected member of its city council, Mr. Aiello is a public
official. (Section 82048; regulation 18701(a).) Further, as a council member, unless
disqualified under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act, Mr. Aiello will make,
participate in making, and influence governmental decisions, including voting on
development projects proposed by Mr. Seeno. (Section 87100; regulation 18702.1 –
18702.3.)

3.      What are Mayor Aiello's economic interests?

The economic interests that might give rise to a conflict of interest are defined in
regulations 18703-18703.5. ³


³ In addition to the economic interests separately listed in section 87103, a public official always
has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, and may have a conflict of interest in any decision
foreseeably resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities of the
official or his or her immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a 12-month period. (Regulation
18703.5.)

File No. I-02-161
Page No. 3

A public official also has an economic interest in any person from whom he or
she has received income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more
within 12 months prior to the governmental decision. (Section 87103(c); regulation
18703.3.) An exception exists when the income consists of a loan to a public official,
made by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official status. (Section 87103(c).) You indicate
that Mayor Aiello received a private loan from the profit sharing plan of Ham Radio
Outlet, Inc. ("HRO"), a business apparently engaged in the sale of electronic products.
This business is not a commercial lending institution and for that reason, the exception
does not apply. (Aguiar Advice Letter, No. I-00-192.) Thus, HRO is a business entity
that is a source of income to Mayor Aiello and, for that reason, he has an economic
interest in HRO.

Identifying a business entity that is a source of income to a public official does
not conclude our analysis. A public official's economic interest in a business entity that
is a source of income to that official will also include an economic interest in parent,
subsidiary, or otherwise related business entities. (Regulation 18703.1(c). Smith Advice
Letter, No. A-00-153.) Should Mr. Seeno's company and HRO have a parent, subsidiary
relationship, or are otherwise related business entities,⁴ then Mr. Seeno's company would
also number among Mayor Aiello's economic interests. There are insufficient facts
presented to allow us to state whether any such relationship exists. Nevertheless, you
should be aware that this possible relationship, if present, could disqualify Mayor Aiello
from participating in city council votes on development projects proposed by Mr. Seeno.

You also indicate that a representative from Mr. Seeno's company referred Mayor
Aiello to Mr. Ferrero, whose business subsequently provided Mayor Aiello with the loan.
In certain limited circumstances, a referring party may be a source of income to a public
official. (See regulation 18703.3(c)(3)(B)(iv).) However, these circumstances are
characterized by the existence of a contract or payment of a referral fee. (Swerdlin
Advice Letter, No. A-02-119; Kazanjian Advice Letter, No. A-92-088.)

There is nothing in the facts you provide to indicate that Mr. Seeno's
representative received a fee in connection with referring the source of the loan to Mayor
Aiello, or that this referral was pursuant to a contractual arrangement between Mr. Seeno
and Mr. Ferrero. Thus, Mr. Seeno is not a source of income to Mayor Aiello as a result
of this referral, and is not, in this regard, an economic interest disqualifying Mayor Aiello
from voting on development projects brought before the city council by Mr. Seeno.⁵

Having answered the question you pose, it is unnecessary to engage in the
remaining five steps of our eight-step standard conflict-of-interest analysis.

⁴ Two business entities are "otherwise related" when any one of three tests is met: a) one
business entity has a controlling ownership in the other; b) shared management and control between the
two entities; and c) the controlling owner of one business entity is also a controlling owner in the other.
(Regulation 18703.1(d)(2)(A)-(C).)
⁵ We have not analyzed Mayor Aiello's economic interest in real property – his principal
residence – based on your representation that he will continue to recuse himself from voting on projects
relating to the Highland Ranch subdivision where his real property is located.

File No. I-02-161
Page No. 4

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Kenneth L. Glick
Counsel, Legal Division
